coredata.dc:title: A facile fluorescence imprinted strategy based on boronic acid functionalized MOF and Mg/N-CDs for discrimination of transferrin: Expansion for boronic acid functionalized MOF application
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Wei
Du, Bin
Pei, Fubin
Liang, Muwen
Yang, Lidong
Liu, Bing
Mu, Xihui
Tong, Zhaoyang
coredata.dc:description: In this research, we proposed a novel fluorescence imprinted sensor (FIS@MIP) for rapid and sensitive detection of transferrin (TrF) based on the boronic acid functionalized Zr-MOF (Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2) and high fluorescence quantum yield of Mg/N co-doped carbon dots (Mg/N-CDs). The boronic acid moieties of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 could significantly covalently bind TrF and load more Mg/N-CDs to avoid fluorescence quenching caused by self-aggregation of Mg/N-CDs. Additionally, Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was prepared though linker fragmentation approach, which eliminated the complex post modification process and could provide sufficient boric acid groups. High luminescent and fluorescence quantum yield endowed Mg/N-CDs remarkable fluorescence response to TrF. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of FIS@MIP at 438 nm was quenched by Trf within 1 min. Based on specific recognition sites, a good linear relationship was obtained in the linear range of 0.1 µM–20 µM, and the low detection limit was determined as 0.067 µM. In order to explore portable detection application, a home-made device based on smartphone was designed for on-site detection of TrF with a detection limit of 0.38 µM. Significantly, this smartphone platform was successfully applied for detection of TrF in simulated actual samples (i.e., human urine and human serum) with satisfactory results, which provided a new promising strategy for on-site and sensitive discrimination of TrF in actual samples. This research will promote the development of the application of boronic-acid functionalized Zr-MOF in molecularly imprinted sensing of glycoproteins..
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transferrin
Boronic-acid functionalized Zr-MOF
Molecularly imprinting
Smartphone sensing
Mg/N-CDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and fluorescence response mechanism
Optimization of experimental conditions of FIS@MIP
Fabrication of the portable smartphone and biosensing performance analysis
Reusability and stability of the FIS@MIP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian (SKLNBC2022-06).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A), (B), (C) and (D) TEM images of Mg/N-CDs, Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP; (E) Elemental mapping images of FIS@MIP. (F) FT-IR spectra of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP; (G) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP; (H) X -ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of FIS@MIP under different excitation. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP with TrF. (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of TrF, excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of FIS@MIP. (E) Fluorescence emission decay curves of FIS@MIP and FIS@MIP + TrF.

(A) and (B) Fluorescence spectra of FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP with different concentrations of TrF (Concentrations of TrF: 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 20 µM). (C) and (D) The linear calibration plot of FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP. (E) The changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio of FIS@MIP towards BHb, BSA, OVA, HRP, Cyt c and TrF. (F) Influence of coexisting interfering proteins (5 µM) on the fluorescence intensity ratio of TrF (1 µM) to FIS@MIP. (Error bar = SD, n = 3).
Schematic diagram of using the developed smartphone-integrated sensing platform to detect TrF. The concentrations of TrF were: 0.4, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 20 μM. (Error bar = SD, n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of FIS@MIP and the discrimination of TrF.
Comparation of other methods for TrF detection.

head.ce:title: A facile fluorescence imprinted strategy based on boronic acid functionalized MOF and Mg/N-CDs for discrimination of transferrin: Expansion for boronic acid functionalized MOF application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, we proposed a novel fluorescence imprinted sensor (FIS@MIP) for rapid and sensitive detection of transferrin (TrF) based on the boronic acid functionalized Zr-MOF (Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2) and high fluorescence quantum yield of Mg/N co-doped carbon dots (Mg/N-CDs). The boronic acid moieties of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 could significantly covalently bind TrF and load more Mg/N-CDs to avoid fluorescence quenching caused by self-aggregation of Mg/N-CDs. Additionally, Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was prepared though linker fragmentation approach, which eliminated the complex post modification process and could provide sufficient boric acid groups. High luminescent and fluorescence quantum yield endowed Mg/N-CDs remarkable fluorescence response to TrF. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of FIS@MIP at 438 nm was quenched by Trf within 1 min. Based on specific recognition sites, a good linear relationship was obtained in the linear range of 0.1 µM–20 µM, and the low detection limit was determined as 0.067 µM. In order to explore portable detection application, a home-made device based on smartphone was designed for on-site detection of TrF with a detection limit of 0.38 µM. Significantly, this smartphone platform was successfully applied for detection of TrF in simulated actual samples (i.e., human urine and human serum) with satisfactory results, which provided a new promising strategy for on-site and sensitive discrimination of TrF in actual samples. This research will promote the development of the application of boronic-acid functionalized Zr-MOF in molecularly imprinted sensing of glycoproteins..

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Glycoproteins, as the main indicators to diseases, have always been the research focus in recent years. The level of glycoproteins in vivo is closely related to the occurrence of some diseases. As a member of glycoproteins family, transferrin (TrF) plays key role in iron distribution and is a biomarker for anemia, diabetes, and malignancies [1]. At present, the strategies designed for TrF detection, such as surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [2], mass spectrometry (MS) [3] and immunosensors [4,5], are useful for the quantitative discrimination of transferrin, but suffer from high cost, complex analytical procedures, and limited storage conditions at the meantime. As an ideal substitution to biosensor using biomolecule as recognition component, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have highly cross-linked internal network structures and imprinted holes of similar size to the targets, which endow them with stable chemistry properties and excellent selectivity characteristics [6]. It was reported that the cis-diol-containing molecules can covalently bind to boric acid groups [7,8]. Some molecularly imprinted sensors based on boric acid as functional monomers were developed for detecting TrF like bamboo shoots springing up after rain, such as MTi-MIP [9], DES-MIPs [10], Fe3O4@TRF-MIPs [11], epitope imprinted PES particles [12], CyA-Fe3O4@TRF-MIPs [13] and Fe3O4@Au@cyclodextrin-MIPs [14]. The above-mentioned strategies based on post-synthetic modification provided a bright future for the development of TrF imprinted sensors. But the challenges about the complicated modification progress and ambiguous modification efficiency of boric acid cannot be ignored.

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs), one kind of porous materials with excellent chemical stability [15] and high surface area,[16] have shown wide promising application in sensing system [17,18]. Especially the tunable functionality makes MOFs potential promising matrix for gas adsorption [19,20], catalysis [21,22], and drug delivery [23]. Currently, boronic acid functionalized MOFs have attracted tremendous attention in bacteria detection [24], eradication of multi-drug-resistant bacteria [25], CO2 adsorption [26] and F- detection [27]. Considering the high affinity between boric acid groups and the molecules containing cis diol structures, the boric acid functionalized MOFs can be a suitable affinity support for glycoproteins enrichment. For example, Li et al. explored a new magnetic boronic-acid MOFs modified via post-synthetic modification for glycoprotein separation [28]. However, boric acid-functionalized MOFs cannot selectively recognize glycoproteins. And few studies have reported that boronic acid-functionalized MOFs could be served as imprinted supporter for glycoproteins detection though literature research. Thus, it would be a meaningful work to design boronic acid-functionalized MOFs as imprinting carriers to explore glycoproteins sensing.

The sensing system with low fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) still suffer from unsatisfactory sensitivity. Mg/N co-doped carbon dots (Mg/N-CDs) designed through magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)2) as chelation agent and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source, possessed high FLQY and have been served as fluorescence signal [29,30] for sensing system for them fascinating physicochemical stability. Meanwhile, the carboxyl groups, formed on the surface of carbon dots by the chelation between magnesium ions and citric acid, are beneficial for enhancing their water solubility. In addition to the signal unit, the method of signal acquisition is also of importance for applying situations. Although the reliable and accurate analysis performance can be obtained based on laboratory instruments, it is also the additional obstacle in fabrication of economical and portable sensing system. The smartphone sensing system integrated with image collection and output functions provide visual readout and portable sensing experience. Smartphone-based fluorescence strategies have been widely used for analysis of various targets [31–33]. While the TrF imprinting sensing method mainly focusing on large laboratory equipment possess excellent selectivity and sensitivity, the strategy toward visual and on-site detection based on molecularly imprinting are barely reported and remain challenges.

Herein, the boronic-acid functionalized Zr-MOF (Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2) synthesized by linker fragmentation approach [26] was first applied to construct fluorescence imprinted sensor (FIS@MIP) for TrF detection. And the high FLQYs of Mg/N-CDs were embedded in imprinted layer and served as luminescent signals. Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was prepared without complex post-synthetic modification. Thanks to the large specific surface area from Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 and high luminescence efficiency from Mg/N-CDs, the designed imprinted sensor exhibited good specificity and sensitivity to TrF. In this work, we focused on on-site detection of TrF, and therefore fabricated a portable smartphone sensing platform. Interestingly, the portable platform integrated with FIS@MIP has successfully realized on-site monitoring TrF in simulated real sample with satisfactory results.
The experimental section was supplemented in the Supplementary Material.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , terephthalic acid (BDC) and 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid (CPB) were served as parent ligand and fragment ligand respectively to synthesize Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 through the hydrothermal method. Mg/N-CDs were served as fluorophores and exhibited fluorescence emission at 438 nm. The large specific surface area of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was first to combine TrF via boronated affinity binding. And then the imprinted layer can be formed on the surface of TrF-immobilized Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 under a certain amount of TEOS (cross-linker) and NH3·H2O (initiator). 10 % sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS) solution was used as eluent to remove template.

The overall morphology of Mg/N-CDs, Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the average size of the Mg/N-CDs were about 5 nm. The morphology of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was shown in Fig. 1B and the average particle size was about 100 nm. Compared to Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, the average particle sizes of FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP were increased to 120 nm (Fig. 1C and 1D), demonstrated that the imprinted layer was successfully introduced into the surface of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2. The elemental mapping images indicated the FIS@MIP contained the elements of B, Mg, N, Si, Zr and O, which was further proof of the successful preparation of the imprinted polymer (Fig. 1E).

To further validate the chemical structure of the obtained materials and the successful modification of imprinted layer, Fourier Translation Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP were investigated (Fig. 1F). The peaks appeared at 1157 cm−1 and 1021 cm−1 were corresponding to the vibrations of B-O-H and B-O-O-H, C-B groups, indicating that CPB was successfully introduced. The peaks between 500 cm−1–800 cm−1 were classical vibration absorption of Zr-O. And the peaks at 1584 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 indicated the presence of O = C-O. The FT-IR spectrum of FIS@MIP was similar to FIS@NIP, in which the peaks at 1088 cm−1 and 799 cm−1, 473 cm−1 were belonged to Si-O-Si and Si-O, respectively. These results indicated that silicon imprinted layer was successfully coated on the surface of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2. FT-IR specturm of Mg/N-CDs was supplemented in Fig. S1, which was corresponding to the previous report [34].

Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms (BET) at 77 K of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP were recorded to assess the textural parameters. From the Fig. 1G, the surface area of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2, FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP were 1224.85 m2/g, 83.2047 m2/g and 73.3896 m2/g, respectively. The surface area of FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP were decreased significantly due to the formation of SiO2-imprinted layer on the surface of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2. As shown in Fig. 1H, XRD patterns of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was consistent with the previous report [35]. Interestingly, only one broad characteristic diffraction peak could be observed at 2θ = 23° in FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP. According to previous reports [36,37], this result was attributed to the thicker imprinted layer of amorphous SiO2 on the surface of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2. Thus, the diffraction peaks of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was obscured by the amorphous components [38]. The above results demonstrated that the imprinted layer of SiO2 was successfully modified on the Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2.

The high-resolution spectrum of C1s, N1s, O1s, Mg1s and Mg2p could be observed in the full-band XPS spectrum of Mg/N-CDs (Fig. S2A), which were consistent with the previous report [34]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. S2B) showed strong signals at 284.28 ev, 258.68 ev and 288.08 ev, which were attributed to the oxygenated carbon atoms (C = C, O = C-O and C = O). From the Fig. S2C, the N1s high-resolution spectrum exhibited three peaks at 399.28 ev (C-N-C), 400.38 ev (N-(C)3) and 401.3 ev (N-H), respectively [39]. The O1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2D) showed two speaks at 530.78 ev (C = O) and 531.38 ev (C-O). The high-resolution spectrum of Mg1s showed single peak at 1303.6 ev, which was assigned to the Mg native oxide (Fig. S2E). Meanwhile, the Mg2p peak at 50.0 ev was Mg-O species (Fig. S2F). Notably, the high-resolution spectrum of Mg Auger peaks (MgKll) accompanying C1s indicated the Mg buried in carbon nuclear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and fluorescence response mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the excitation of FIS@MIP in the range of 300 nm–400 nm was investigated, and the optimal excitation was 360 nm. From Fig. 2B, FIS@NIP exhibited analogous fluorescence emission to Mg/N-CDs at 438 nm. However, compared with FIS@MIP, the fluorescence intensity of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was weak, which demonstrated that the influence of the fluorescence of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was negligible for entire research. To verify the feasibility of FIS@MIP for TrF detection, the fluorescence intensity changes before and after adding the TrF were studied. The fluorescence emission of FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP at 438 nm were all quenched after adding same concentration of TrF, but FIS@MIP exhibited more obvious fluorescence quenching (Fig. 2C). This phenomenon indicated that this strategy based on FIS@MIP could be used for discrimination of TrF. Recently, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms are mainly internal filtering effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and static quenching [40]. To investegated the fluorescence quenching mechanism of this work, UV–vis absorption spectrum of TrF, excitation spectrum and fluorescence emission of FIS@MIP were researched. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, there was no obvious overlap between the absorption spectrum of TrF and fluorescence emission of FIS@MIP, which proved that the quenching mechanism was not FRET and IFE [41]. Thus, the fluorescence quenching may be caused by static quenching and PET. Static quenching will occur when complex is formed between the quencher and the fluorophore at ground state, and the static quenching has no impact on lifetime of the fluorophore [42]. As shown in Fig. 2E, the fluorescence lifetime of FIS@MIP decreased from 11.41 ns to 11.02 ns after addition of TrF, indicating that the quenching mechanism was not static queching. Thus, the fluorescence quenching can only be attributed to the PET. Based on the pevious literature [43], the affinity reaction of boric acid of FIS@MIP with the cis-diol of TrF can shorten the distance of TrF to FIS@MIP, resulting in the excited electrons from Mg/N-CDs to the conduction band were transferred directly to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital UV energy level of TrF. Based on the above analysis, the fluorescence quenching was caused by PET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions of FIS@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: It is of great significance to optimize imprinted influence factors for superior specific response ability of FIS@MIP for TrF. Therefore, some comparative experiments were performed to determine the optimal conditions. The amount of cross-linker is closely related to the thickness of the imprinted layer. The imprinted polymer with too thick imprinted layer can increase the difficulty in eluting template molecules and influence the binding efficiency to target. In contrast, if the imprinted layer too thin to coat protein, specific recognition ability stems from the shape and size of the recognition sites will be weakened. The amount of TEOS and the imprinted time used in this work were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3A, the highest fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) appeared at 150 µL, indicating that the thickness of imprinted layer was suitable for future experiments at 150 µL of TEOS. Meanwhile, the highest fluorescence intensity ratio of FIS@MIP appeared at 12 h and decreased as the imprinted time prolonged (Fig. S3B). These results indicated the binding efficiency reached highest when the amount of TEOS and the imprinted time were 150 µL and 12 h, respectively. A propriate amount of Mg/N-CDs will affect the sensing sensitivity. As shown in Fig. S3C, F0/F value of FIS@MIP reached the peak when the amount of Mg/N-CDs was 20 mg, indicating that the response efficiency of FIS@MIP to TrF was highest at this concentration.

To obtain accuracy detection and excellent sensing performance of FIS@MIP, the detection parameters about the pH of detection environment, the concentration of FIS@MIP and absorption time were optimized. The pH of detection environment can affect the TrF recognition capability of FIS@MIP, and both acidity and alkalinity can affect the activity of TrF. From the Fig. S3D, the fluorescence quenching ratio enhanced with the increase of pH from 6–7.5, but decreased with the increase of pH from 7.5–9. Thus, the optimized pH was set at 7.5. As illustrated in Fig. S3E, the value of F0/F increased with the increasing of the concentration of TrF, and reached highest at 0.5 mg/mL. To obtain sufficient response time, the incubation time was optimized. From the picture, the value of F0/F reached equilibrium at 1 min (Fig. S3F). This rapid response was attributed to tailor-made recognition sites in FIS@MIP.

To verify the fluorescence performance of imprinted polymer for TrF, the fluorescence intensity changes of FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP at 438 nm were researched under same concentration range of TrF. Given by the optimal experimental conditions, FIS@MIP displayed more obvious fluorescence quenching over the same concentrations ranging from 0.1 µM–20 µM (Fig. 3 A and 3B). The Stern-Volmer equation of (F0-F) / F = KSV ·CTrF was used to described the relationship of F0/F and the concentration of TrF, where KSV was the fluorescence quenching constant, F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of FIS@MIP in the absence and presence of TrF, respectively. And CTrF was the concentration of TrF. As illustrated in Fig. 3C and 3D, the fluorescence intensity of FIS@MIP exhibited excellent linear relationship with different concentration of TrF. And the corresponding standard linear equations of FIS@MIP and FIS@NIP were y = 0.0402CTrF + 1.165 and y = 0.0144CTrF + 1.047, respectively. Based on the standard 3σ/S [44,45], the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.067 µM. Imprinting factor (IF) could reflect the imprinting effect of materials to a certain extent, which was calculated by the formula: IF = Ksv,MIP / Ksv,NIP. Based on the equation, the imprinted factor was determined as 2.79, which demonstrated that FIS@MIP enjoyed excellent recognition capability to TrF. Compared with FIS@NIP, FIS@MIP exhibited obvious fluorescence color changes under UV light (28 W) (Fig. 3A and 3B). The mentioned results demonstrated that FIS@MIP possessed cavity structures that were more compatible with the TrF.

The FLQYs of Mg/N-CDs, N-CDs, FIS@MIP and N-doped carbon quantum dots-embedded imprinted polymer (N-CDs@UiO-66-B(OH)2@MIP) were shown in Table S1, and the fluorescence spectra of Mg/N-CDs and N-CDs were shown in Fig. S4. From the picture, the fluorescence intensity of Mg/N-CDs was obviously stronger than that of N-CDs, which was consistent with the quantum yield test results. In order to demonstrate the high FLQY of Mg/N-CDs possess remarkable fluorescence response to TrF. The fluorescence responsiveness of FIS@MIP and N-CDs@UiO-66-B(OH)2@MIP to TrF was conducted. As shown in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@UiO-66-B(OH)2@MIP decreased dramatically under the same concentrations of TrF. However, the fluorescence quenching tendency of FIS@MIP was obvious stronger than that of N-CDs@UiO-66-B(OH)2@MIP (Fig. S5B). This phenomenon indicated the Mg/N-CDs with high FLQY endowed FIS@MIP more excellent optical properties, resulting in higher sensitivity.

Several analogous proteins, such as bovine hemoglobin (BHb), bovine albumin (BSA), ovalbumin (OVA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and cytochrome c (Cyt c), were selected as the interferences to validate the specific recognition of FIS@MIP. The response time was set as 1 min. As illustrated in Fig. 3E, the value of F0/F was highest under the circumstance of TrF. It was noted that OVA and HRP all were glycoproteins, which contained cis-diol structures, but displayed negligible response. These results demonstrated that the tailor-made cavities which were complementary to the size and shape of TrF in FIS@MIP played important role in target discrimination. Accordingly, FIS@MIP possessed satisfactory screening function for TrF, and had strong affinity and specific recognition of TrF. Based on previous report [7], the cis-diol containing molecules can covalently bind to boric acid groups and form cyclic boronated esters. Meanwhile, the binding energy will change after the absorption of analytes [46]. To investigated the principle of specific recognition of FIS@MIP toward TrF. The XPS spectra about B element of FIS@MIP before and after TrF addition were recorded. As shown in Fig. S6, the peak of B 1 s (FIS@MIP + TrF) at 199.2 eV was higher than that of FIS@MIP (198.0 eV), indicating that there was affinity between FIS@MIP and TrF. Thus, based on above analysis and the specificity research, the high selectivity of FIS@MIP toward TrF was attributed to the boric acid groups of FIS@MIP.

The competitive experiments of FIS@MIP were conductive by introducing BHb, BSA, OVA, HRP and Cyt c. As shown in Fig. 3F, there was no significant fluorescence intensity ratio change for FIS@MIP, demonstrating that FIS@MIP possessed excellent recognition ability for TrF due to the tailor-made sites. To further investigate the anti-interference ability of FIS@MIP, some biologically important molecules, such as cysteine (Csy), homocysteine (Hcy), creatinine (Cre), aspartic acid (Asp), homocysteine (Hcy) and glucose were selected as interferences. As shown in Fig. S7, these biologically molecules had weak binding performance with the imprinted cavities of FIS@MIP, resulting in little change in fluorescence intensity ratio of FIS@MIP. These results demonstrated that FIS@MIP had excellent selectivity towards TrF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the portable smartphone and biosensing performance analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence assay conducted in laboratory cannot meet on-site monitor requirement. And the slight changes in fluorescence color are not sufficient to realize sensitive discrimination of TrF. Given such typical shortcomings, a portable smartphone sensing system was fabricated for on-site detection of TrF. The homemade facile device consists of three parts: ultraviolet source (UV light with 365 nm and power source), sample cell (sample slot and cuvette) and signal receiving device (optical filer and smartphone) (Fig. S8). The Color Picker app was used to extract the RGB values from the fluorescence pictures obtained by smartphone and converted them into grayscale values (I). Based on the previous reports [47–49], the formula I = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B was applied to determine the grayscale values. The same as the fluorescence detection method based on fluorometer, the concentration of FIS@MIP and response time were set as 0.5 mg/mL and 1 min. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence images depended on the TrF concentrations was acquired by the smartphone sensing system. The relationship of Imean and the concentrations of TrF (0.4–20 μM) can be denoted by Imean = 0.0077CTrF + 0.0172 (R2 = 0.934), in which Imean (the mean value) was given by the equation Imean = (Iblank - Itest )/Iblank . The LOD was determined as 0.38 μM based on the standard 3σ/slope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability of the FIS@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Excellent reusability, reproducibility and stability of sensors are of importance for the application of complex detection environments. The reusability was evaluated through five times of removal-rebinding cycles by detecting the fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S9A, the fluorescence intensity changed insignificantly, indicating that the developed nanomaterials possessed good circular application capability. And the response performance of FIS@MIP to TrF was in the same batch (Fig. S9B). After being stored 30 days at 4 ℃, the fluorescence intensity of FIS@MIP had no obviously changes (Fig. S9C). These results demonstrated that the designed nanomaterials possessed potential for practical application.

In order to test the practical analysis ability of FIS@MIP, commercially human urine and human serum were served as simulated actual samples, and the TrF spiked concentrations were listed in Table S2. The recoveries from the human urine and human serum ranging from 87.1 %–100.8 %, and the RSD was < 5.32 %. The results obtained through HPLC-UV method were corresponding to the above results, which demonstrated that the sensing system based on FIS@MIP could be adapted to meet the requirements for accurate analysis of TrF in actual samples. The practicability of the smartphone-based FIS@MIP was apprised. As shown in Table S3, Delightfully, the spiked recoveries of human urine and human serum were 81.8 %–96.5 %, and the RSD was < 5.37 %, which were consistent with results obtained by fluorescence method based on FIS@MIP. These results indicated that the constructed portable smartphone sensing platform had promising applications for TrF discrimination.

The sensing method based on FIS@MIP was compared with other strategy for TrF analysis. As listed in Table 1 , the designed fluorescent approach in this work exhibited fast fluorescence response (1 min) to TrF with low limit of detection (0.067 μM) under a wide linear range (0.1 μM–20 μM). Compared with other methods, this method provided a novel imprinted strategy for TrF without complex post synthetic modification of boric acid functional group. Meantime, a smartphone sensing platform was applied for on-site detecting TrF, providing potential possibility for real-time and on-site detection in the future. The obvious advantages of fluorescence methods could be attributed to the following aspects. Firstly, Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 was covalently bound to the TrF, which was conducive to the formation of an imprinted layer on the surface. Under the optimal imprinting conditions, imprinted cavities that matched the size and shape of TrF could be formed. Secondly, Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 could serve as imprinted supporter to load more Mg/N-CDs, which was benefited for improving the sensitivity of the sensing system. Additionally, the high FLQY of Mg/N-CDs could improve response sensitivity of FIS@MIP. In addition, the smartphone sensing platform was applied for on-site detecting TrF, which proposed a simple and accurate method for TrF discrimination.

In this work, Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 and Mg/N-CDs were used to construct imprinted sensor for TrF analysis. And given by the excellent merits of Zr-UiO-66-B(OH)2 and Mg/N-CDs, the fluorescence nanosensor based on FIS@MIP can rapidly response TrF within 1 min, and exhibited good sensitivity (0.067 μM) and selectivity. The favorable realistic application was validated by the successful application of the fluorescence method-based FIS@MIP in simulated actual samples. In addition. With integrated smartphone sensing strategy, the platform based on FIS@MIP exhibited good detection performance for TrF with the LOD of 0.38 μM. The current work realized reasonable design of imprinted sensor for TrF based on boronic acid functionalized MOF, but also furnished application for on-site and visual discrimination of TrF.

Wei Hu: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Bin Du: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Fubin Pei: Software, Validation. Muwen Liang: Resources. Lidong Yang: Resources. Bing Liu: Visualization, Funding acquisition. Xihui Mu: Resources. Zhaoyang Tong: Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian (SKLNBC2022-06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109759.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
